title: Forging the Path to Arydia (unboxing, organizing, and the learning process)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=SB5QPpcWDXk

Daniel (@dungeondive) takes a preliminary look at Arydia: The Paths We Dare Tread, Cody Miller's 'RPG in a box' from Far Off Games (of Xia fame) — part unboxing, part prep session ahead of his first game with friends, because he's convinced this one rewards homework before you sit down. The box is colossal and, unusually, packed to the last millimetre. He marvels at the overproduction: triple-layer punch-out hex maps you press to flip, prepainted minis with swappable heads matched to your portrait, and chunky metal coins. His lone gripe is the flimsy per-class treasure chests. Two things delight him most: minimal in-play chits, since most setup happens once, up front; and — crucially — no giant campaign book, with all story and NPC text confined to concise poker-sized cards, sparing his group the mid-game reading slog they hated in Middara. He walks through building his character, Malik the Plucky, a human Path-of-Valor warrior, slotting portrait, stats, skills and gear into a satisfying board with a paper-doll equipment layout and a two-step unlock-then-master skill tree. The premise — exiles collecting 'squills' to buy pardons home — frames a sandbox campaign of jobs and exploration he's very excited to play.

----

Arydia puts all its story text on concise cards instead of a fat campaign tome. For a narrative adventure, do you prefer bite-sized card text that keeps play moving, or a rich book you can sink into between turns?
